g.data-narration: Welcome to Cloud Computing Service Models. We'll explore NIST definitions and the SPI framework.
Cloud computing delivers computing resources as on-demand services over the Internet on a pay-per-use basis.
g.text: A paradigm shift delivering computing resources (servers, storage,
applications) as on-demand services from providers like AWS, Azure, GCP.
Instead of owning infrastructure, access resources over the Internet on pay-per-use basis.

g.data-narration: NIST defines five essential characteristics of cloud computing: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.
g.text: Auto-provisioning, network access, multi-tenant pooling, dynamic scaling, and usage-based metering.

g.data-narration: The SPI model consists of three service layers: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS, each building upon the layer below.
g.text: The SPI Model: Three Service Layers

g.data-narration: IaaS provides virtualized computing resources. You manage the operating system, runtime, and applications while the provider handles the underlying infrastructure.
g.text: You build the house; provider maintains land

g.data-narration: PaaS provides a development platform. You manage only your applications and data while the provider supplies the operating system, runtime, and infrastructure.
g.text: Building, furniture provided; bring your belongings

g.data-narration: SaaS delivers complete software applications over the web. You only manage user settings and data while the provider handles everything else.
g.text: Just get in and go; driver handles everything

g.data-narration: This comparison table shows the key differences: IaaS offers high user control, PaaS provides built-in auto-scalability, and SaaS is ready-made software with provider-handled maintenance.
Beyond SPI, new models emerge: FaaS for serverless computing, DBaaS for managed databases, and CaaS for container orchestration services.
Cloud deployment models include Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community clouds, each offering different levels of control, security, and resource sharing.
Key takeaways: NIST defines five characteristics. As you move from IaaS to SaaS, user control decreases while provider management increases. Choose based on your specific needs.

g.text: NIST defines 5 characteristics: On-demand, Broad access, Pooling, Elasticity, Measured
IaaS to PaaS to SaaS: User control decreases, provider management increases
Select model based on needs: IaaS equals max control, PaaS equals dev productivity
Deployment: Public, Private, Hybrid, Community - choose based on security and compliance
